"""
Components for parsing variable assignments and internally representing plot data.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

from collections.abc import Mapping, Sized
from typing import cast

import pandas as pd
from pandas import DataFrame

from seaborn._core.typing import DataSource, VariableSpec, ColumnName


class PlotData:
    """
    Data table with plot variable schema and mapping to original names.

    Contains logic for parsing variable specification arguments and updating
    the table with layer-specific data and/or mappings.

    Parameters
    ----------
    data
        Input data where variable names map to vector values.
    variables
        Keys are names of plot variables (x, y, ...) each value is one of:

        - name of a column (or index level, or dictionary entry) in `data`
        - vector in any format that can construct a :class:`pandas.DataFrame`

    Attributes
    ----------
    frame
        Data table with column names having defined plot variables.
    names
        Dictionary mapping plot variable names to names in source data structure(s).
    ids
        Dictionary mapping plot variable names to unique data source identifiers.

    """
    frame: DataFrame
    frames: dict[tuple, DataFrame]
    names: dict[str, str | None]
    ids: dict[str, str | int]
    source_data: DataSource
    source_vars: dict[str, VariableSpec]

    def __init__(
        self,
        data: DataSource,
        variables: dict[str, VariableSpec],
    ):

        frame, names, ids = self._assign_variables(data, variables)

        self.frame = frame
        self.names = names
        self.ids = ids

        self.frames = {}  # TODO this is a hack, remove

        self.source_data = data
        self.source_vars = variables

    def __contains__(self, key: str) -> bool:
        """Boolean check on whether a variable is defined in this dataset."""
        if self.frame is None:
            return any(key in df for df in self.frames.values())
        return key in self.frame

    def join(
        self,
        data: DataSource,
        variables: dict[str, VariableSpec] | None,
    ) -> PlotData:
        """Add, replace, or drop variables and return as a new dataset."""
        # Inherit the original source of the upsteam data by default
        if data is None:
            data = self.source_data

        # TODO allow `data` to be a function (that is called on the source data?)

        if not variables:
            variables = self.source_vars

        # Passing var=None implies that we do not want that variable in this layer
        disinherit = [k for k, v in variables.items() if v is None]

        # Create a new dataset with just the info passed here
        new = PlotData(data, variables)

        # -- Update the inherited DataSource with this new information

        drop_cols = [k for k in self.frame if k in new.frame or k in disinherit]
        parts = [self.frame.drop(columns=drop_cols), new.frame]

        # Because we are combining distinct columns, this is perhaps more
        # naturally thought of as a "merge"/"join". But using concat because
        # some simple testing suggests that it is marginally faster.
        frame = pd.concat(parts, axis=1, sort=False, copy=False)

        names = {k: v for k, v in self.names.items() if k not in disinherit}
        names.update(new.names)

        ids = {k: v for k, v in self.ids.items() if k not in disinherit}
        ids.update(new.ids)

        new.frame = frame
        new.names = names
        new.ids = ids

        # Multiple chained operations should always inherit from the original object
        new.source_data = self.source_data
        new.source_vars = self.source_vars

        return new

    def _assign_variables(
        self,
        data: DataSource,
        variables: dict[str, VariableSpec],
    ) -> tuple[DataFrame, dict[str, str | None], dict[str, str | int]]:
        """
        Assign values for plot variables given long-form data and/or vector inputs.

        Parameters
        ----------
        data
            Input data where variable names map to vector values.
        variables
            Keys are names of plot variables (x, y, ...) each value is one of:

            - name of a column (or index level, or dictionary entry) in `data`
            - vector in any format that can construct a :class:`pandas.DataFrame`

        Returns
        -------
        frame
            Table mapping seaborn variables (x, y, color, ...) to data vectors.
        names
            Keys are defined seaborn variables; values are names inferred from
            the inputs (or None when no name can be determined).
        ids
            Like the `names` dict, but `None` values are replaced by the `id()`
            of the data object that defined the variable.

        Raises
        ------
        ValueError
            When variables are strings that don't appear in `data`, or when they are
            non-indexed vector datatypes that have a different length from `data`.

        """
        source_data: Mapping | DataFrame
        frame: DataFrame
        names: dict[str, str | None]
        ids: dict[str, str | int]

        plot_data = {}
        names = {}
        ids = {}

        given_data = data is not None
        if data is not None:
            source_data = data
        else:
            # Data is optional; all variables can be defined as vectors
            # But simplify downstream code by always having a usable source data object
            source_data = {}

        # TODO Generally interested in accepting a generic DataFrame interface
        # Track https://data-apis.org/ for development

        # Variables can also be extracted from the index of a DataFrame
        if isinstance(source_data, pd.DataFrame):
            index = source_data.index.to_frame().to_dict("series")
        else:
            index = {}

        for key, val in variables.items():

            # Simply ignore variables with no specification
            if val is None:
                continue

            # Try to treat the argument as a key for the data collection.
            # But be flexible about what can be used as a key.
            # Usually it will be a string, but allow other hashables when
            # taking from the main data object. Allow only strings to reference
            # fields in the index, because otherwise there is too much ambiguity.

            # TODO this will be rendered unnecessary by the following pandas fix:
            # https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/pull/41283
            try:
                hash(val)
                val_is_hashable = True
            except TypeError:
                val_is_hashable = False

            val_as_data_key = (
                # See https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/pull/41283
                # (isinstance(val, abc.Hashable) and val in source_data)
                (val_is_hashable and val in source_data)
                or (isinstance(val, str) and val in index)
            )

            if val_as_data_key:
                val = cast(ColumnName, val)
                if val in source_data:
                    plot_data[key] = source_data[val]
                elif val in index:
                    plot_data[key] = index[val]
                names[key] = ids[key] = str(val)

            elif isinstance(val, str):

                # This looks like a column name but, lookup failed.

                err = f"Could not interpret value `{val}` for `{key}`. "
                if not given_data:
                    err += "Value is a string, but `data` was not passed."
                else:
                    err += "An entry with this name does not appear in `data`."
                raise ValueError(err)

            else:

                # Otherwise, assume the value somehow represents data

                # Ignore empty data structures
                if isinstance(val, Sized) and len(val) == 0:
                    continue

                # If vector has no index, it must match length of data table
                if isinstance(data, pd.DataFrame) and not isinstance(val, pd.Series):
                    if isinstance(val, Sized) and len(data) != len(val):
                        val_cls = val.__class__.__name__
                        err = (
                            f"Length of {val_cls} vectors must match length of `data`"
                            f" when both are used, but `data` has length {len(data)}"
                            f" and the vector passed to `{key}` has length {len(val)}."
                        )
                        raise ValueError(err)

                plot_data[key] = val

                # Try to infer the original name using pandas-like metadata
                if hasattr(val, "name"):
                    names[key] = ids[key] = str(val.name)  # type: ignore  # mypy/1424
                else:
                    names[key] = None
                    ids[key] = id(val)

        # Construct a tidy plot DataFrame. This will convert a number of
        # types automatically, aligning on index in case of pandas objects
        # TODO Note: this fails when variable specs *only* have scalars!
        frame = pd.DataFrame(plot_data)

        return frame, names, ids

Thought: Plan / first steps
- I will examine the stack trace and the likely call path to find where boolean data ends up being treated as continuous numeric data.
- I inspected the scale setup code (ContinuousBase._setup) where the exception arises and then inspected how plot properties choose scales (Property.default_scale and Color.infer_scale).
- I identified the root cause and prepared a minimal, complete fix. I list all files / locations that need to change and give the exact code to add.

What I inspected and why
- seaborn/_core/scales.py: ContinuousBase._setup — where forward(vmax) - forward(vmin) is computed and the TypeError arises when forward returns boolean (boolean - boolean not supported).
- seaborn/_core/plot.py: Plotter._setup_scales and Plot._get_scale — to see how a property's scale is selected when a data vector is provided.
- seaborn/_core/properties.py: Property.default_scale, Color.infer_scale and related mapping logic — to see how Color is defaulting to a Continuous scale for boolean data in the problematic case.
- seaborn/_core/rules.py: variable_type — to see how booleans are classified.

Root cause (concise)
- When the user passes color=[True, False] (a boolean vector), Plot._get_scale calls prop.default_scale(values) because the user didn't pass an explicit scale object or scale-arg. The generic Property.default_scale calls variable_type(data, boolean_type="numeric") — i.e., it treats a boolean-only vector as "numeric" by default. This causes a Continuous scale to be chosen for the color property.
- ContinuousBase._setup builds a normalization function that applies the continuous forward transformation to vmin and vmax and computes b = forward(vmax) - forward(vmin). Since vmin and vmax are booleans, forward returns boolean arrays and subtraction raises a TypeError.
- Note: Color.infer_scale already used variable_type(..., boolean_type="categorical") (so when the user passed a scale-argument string etc., Color treated booleans as categorical correctly), but default_scale (used here) was still the base implementation that treated booleans as numeric. This inconsistency is the direct reason for the crash.

Complete fix (what to change and why)
- Change: Override default_scale in the Color property class so that when seaborn is inferring a scale from the data (i.e., no explicit scale argument was passed), boolean-valued data will be treated as categorical for color mapping.
- File to change: seaborn/_core/properties.py — add a default_scale method to the Color class.
- Rationale: Color mappings (discrete palettes) are usually the expected mapping for boolean data; choosing a nominal scale for boolean color data avoids trying to run continuous transforms on booleans.

Exact code to add
- In seaborn/_core/properties.py, inside the Color class (class Color(Property):), add the following method:

    def default_scale(self, data: Series) -> Scale:
        """Given data, initialize appropriate scale class for color variables.

        Treat boolean-like data as categorical so that boolean vectors such
        as [True, False] get a nominal palette rather than a continuous cmap.
        """
        # For color semantics, booleans should act like categorical data
        var_type = variable_type(data, boolean_type="categorical")
        if var_type == "numeric":
            return Continuous()
        elif var_type == "datetime":
            return Temporal()
        else:
            return Nominal()

Notes on imports / symbols
- Continuous, Temporal and Nominal are already imported near the top of properties.py:
    from seaborn._core.scales import Scale, Nominal, Continuous, Temporal
  so no additional imports are needed.

Why this is a complete fix
- The failure path was: Plot._get_scale -> prop.default_scale(values) -> Property.default_scale -> Continuous() selected -> ContinuousBase._setup -> op on booleans -> TypeError.
- By overriding Color.default_scale we intercept the selection step and ensure Nominal is returned when the data are booleans; this prevents Continuous scale setup from attempting numeric arithmetic on boolean arrays.
- This change also benefits any aliases that use Color (for example, fillcolor and edgecolor are mapped to Color in PROPERTIES) so they won't suffer the same crash.

Other related points / possible followups (not required to fix this crash but worth noting)
- There is a comment / TODO in Fill.get_mapping about ordering of boolean categories: categorical_order returns [False, True] for booleans while Fill._default_values produces [True, False] by default. This is a minor inconsistency in ordering for boolean category levels; it does not cause the crash but produces counterintuitive mapping order. Consider aligning categorical_order or Fill._default_values in a follow-up PR if desired.
- categorical_order sorts levels when they look numeric. That behavior was left unchanged. The fix limits the change to the Color property so we don't alter other property defaults unpredictably.
- Tests to add:
  - A regression test that calling Plot(..., color=[True, False]) does not raise and that the output uses two discrete colors (e.g., check that the color scale is a Nominal scale and that the mapped colors are finite RGB values).
  - Optionally, tests for fillcolor / edgecolor with boolean data.

Summary of files changed
- Modified: seaborn/_core/properties.py
  - Add Color.default_scale(...) as above.

Why no other files need to change
- The crash occurs because of scale selection for Color when no explicit scale was supplied; fixing Color.default_scale addresses all places where color data is inferred from a boolean data vector (including other properties that alias to Color such as fillcolor/edgecolor). The lower-level scale code (ContinuousBase._setup) is correct for continuous data; the bug was a mistaken choice of continuous scale for boolean color values.

If you want, I can:
- Produce a small patch/diff for the repository with the exact insertion in properties.py.
- Add a regression test to the tests suite to lock this behavior in.

bug_report.Title: Color mapping crashes when given boolean data (TypeError in ContinuousBase._setup)
bug_report.Description: Passing a boolean vector for a semantic color argument causes a TypeError during scale setup because the code selects a continuous scale and performs numeric operations on boolean values.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) seaborn/_core/scales.py: ContinuousBase._setup — computes b = forward(vmax) - forward(vmin). This expression assumes forward(vmax) and forward(vmin) are numeric; when they are boolean, numpy raises a TypeError ('numpy boolean subtract ... is not supported').

2) seaborn/_core/properties.py: Property.default_scale (used when no explicit scale is provided) calls variable_type(data, boolean_type='numeric'), i.e., it treats a boolean-only data vector as numeric. This causes a Continuous scale to be selected for boolean color data, which is only valid if booleans are being treated as numeric.

3) seaborn/_core/properties.py: Color.infer_scale (different code-path used when inferring from other inputs) uses variable_type(..., boolean_type='categorical'), creating an inconsistency: the generic default path treats booleans as numeric while Color-specific inference treats them as categorical. This inconsistent boolean_type choice is what allows boolean color values to be mapped to a Continuous scale in the default path.

4) seaborn/_core/plot.py: Plotter._setup_scales / Plot._get_scale — when the plot machinery calls prop.default_scale(values) for the color property (no explicit scale provided), the combination of (2) and (1) produces the crash. The root problem is the incorrect assumption encoded in Property.default_scale that boolean data should be considered numeric for default scale selection.

5) Correct relationship for color semantics (diagnostic conclusion supported by the investigation): boolean-valued data supplied for a color semantic should be treated as categorical/nominal (i.e., boolean_type='categorical') so that a nominal (discrete) color scale is chosen rather than a Continuous scale.
StepsToReproduce: Run the following minimal example in a seaborn-based environment:
Observe that a TypeError is raised during scale setup.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Providing a boolean vector for a color semantic maps the two boolean levels to two discrete colors (a nominal/ categorical color mapping) and produces the plot without raising an exception.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Seaborn raises a TypeError from numpy boolean subtract during scale setup (originating from b = forward(vmax) - forward(vmin) in ContinuousBase._setup) because the default scale selection chose a Continuous scale for boolean color data.

class VariableType(UserString):
    """
    Prevent comparisons elsewhere in the library from using the wrong name.

    Errors are simple assertions because users should not be able to trigger
    them. If that changes, they should be more verbose.

    """
    # TODO we can replace this with typing.Literal on Python 3.8+
    allowed = "numeric", "datetime", "categorical"

    def __init__(self, data):
        assert data in self.allowed, data
        super().__init__(data)

    def __eq__(self, other):
        assert other in self.allowed, other
        return self.data == other


def variable_type(vector, boolean_type="numeric"):
    """
    Determine whether a vector contains numeric, categorical, or datetime data.

    This function differs from the pandas typing API in two ways:

    - Python sequences or object-typed PyData objects are considered numeric if
      all of their entries are numeric.
    - String or mixed-type data are considered categorical even if not
      explicitly represented as a :class:`pandas.api.types.CategoricalDtype`.

    Parameters
    ----------
    vector : :func:`pandas.Series`, :func:`numpy.ndarray`, or Python sequence
        Input data to test.
    boolean_type : 'numeric' or 'categorical'
        Type to use for vectors containing only 0s and 1s (and NAs).

    Returns
    -------
    var_type : 'numeric', 'categorical', or 'datetime'
        Name identifying the type of data in the vector.
    """
    vector = pd.Series(vector)

    # If a categorical dtype is set, infer categorical
    if isinstance(vector.dtype, pd.CategoricalDtype):
        return VariableType("categorical")

    # Special-case all-na data, which is always "numeric"
    if pd.isna(vector).all():
        return VariableType("numeric")

    # At this point, drop nans to simplify further type inference
    vector = vector.dropna()

    # Special-case binary/boolean data, allow caller to determine
    # This triggers a numpy warning when vector has strings/objects
    # https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/6784
    # Because we reduce with .all(), we are agnostic about whether the
    # comparison returns a scalar or vector, so we will ignore the warning.
    # It triggers a separate DeprecationWarning when the vector has datetimes:
    # https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/13548
    # This is considered a bug by numpy and will likely go away.
    with warnings.catch_warnings():
        warnings.simplefilter(
            action='ignore', category=(FutureWarning, DeprecationWarning)
        )
        if np.isin(vector, [0, 1]).all():
            return VariableType(boolean_type)

    # Defer to positive pandas tests
    if pd.api.types.is_numeric_dtype(vector):
        return VariableType("numeric")

    if pd.api.types.is_datetime64_dtype(vector):
        return VariableType("datetime")

    # --- If we get to here, we need to check the entries

    # Check for a collection where everything is a number

    def all_numeric(x):
        for x_i in x:
            if not isinstance(x_i, Number):
                return False
        return True

    if all_numeric(vector):
        return VariableType("numeric")

    # Check for a collection where everything is a datetime

    def all_datetime(x):
        for x_i in x:
            if not isinstance(x_i, (datetime, np.datetime64)):
                return False
        return True

    if all_datetime(vector):
        return VariableType("datetime")

    # Otherwise, our final fallback is to consider things categorical

    return VariableType("categorical")


def infer_orient(x=None, y=None, orient=None, require_numeric=True):
    """Determine how the plot should be oriented based on the data.

    For historical reasons, the convention is to call a plot "horizontally"
    or "vertically" oriented based on the axis representing its dependent
    variable. Practically, this is used when determining the axis for
    numerical aggregation.

    Parameters
    ----------
    x, y : Vector data or None
        Positional data vectors for the plot.
    orient : string or None
        Specified orientation. If not None, can be "x" or "y", or otherwise
        must start with "v" or "h".
    require_numeric : bool
        If set, raise when the implied dependent variable is not numeric.

    Returns
    -------
    orient : "x" or "y"

    Raises
    ------
    ValueError: When `orient` is an unknown string.
    TypeError: When dependent variable is not numeric, with `require_numeric`

    """

    x_type = None if x is None else variable_type(x)
    y_type = None if y is None else variable_type(y)

    nonnumeric_dv_error = "{} orientation requires numeric `{}` variable."
    single_var_warning = "{} orientation ignored with only `{}` specified."

    if x is None:
        if str(orient).startswith("h"):
            warnings.warn(single_var_warning.format("Horizontal", "y"))
        if require_numeric and y_type != "numeric":
            raise TypeError(nonnumeric_dv_error.format("Vertical", "y"))
        return "x"

    elif y is None:
        if str(orient).startswith("v"):
            warnings.warn(single_var_warning.format("Vertical", "x"))
        if require_numeric and x_type != "numeric":
            raise TypeError(nonnumeric_dv_error.format("Horizontal", "x"))
        return "y"

    elif str(orient).startswith("v") or orient == "x":
        if require_numeric and y_type != "numeric":
            raise TypeError(nonnumeric_dv_error.format("Vertical", "y"))
        return "x"

    elif str(orient).startswith("h") or orient == "y":
        if require_numeric and x_type != "numeric":
            raise TypeError(nonnumeric_dv_error.format("Horizontal", "x"))
        return "y"

    elif orient is not None:
        err = (
            "`orient` must start with 'v' or 'h' or be None, "
            f"but `{repr(orient)}` was passed."
        )
        raise ValueError(err)

    elif x_type != "categorical" and y_type == "categorical":
        return "y"

    elif x_type != "numeric" and y_type == "numeric":
        return "x"

    elif x_type == "numeric" and y_type != "numeric":
        return "y"

    elif require_numeric and "numeric" not in (x_type, y_type):
        err = "Neither the `x` nor `y` variable appears to be numeric."
        raise TypeError(err)

    else:
        return "x"

Plan / what I'll do
- I will explain the root cause (why the KeyError happens with MultiIndex columns).
- I will list every location in seaborn that must be changed to correctly index DataFrames using MultiIndex column labels (not just the obviously failing line).

I inspected seaborn's code to find the code paths implicated by the stack trace you posted and to find all places where DataFrame columns are selected using bracket indexing with variable keys that might be MultiIndex tuples.

What I found (summary)
- Root cause: code uses DataFrame.__getitem__ (square-bracket indexing) with keys that can be tuples. pandas.DataFrame.__getitem__ treats tuples as iterables and will convert them to lists (attempting to select multiple columns) rather than treating them as a single MultiIndex key. For example, data[('A', '1')] is treated as data[ ['A', '1'] ] (i.e., attempt to select two columns 'A' and '1'), which leads to KeyError "'1' not in index" when columns are a MultiIndex of tuples. The safe way to select a single column label that is a tuple in a MultiIndex columns DataFrame is to use .loc[:, key] (or .xs, or .loc with indexers) rather than the ambiguous __getitem__ path.
- The stack trace you showed points to axisgrid.PairGrid.map_diag -> vector = self.data[var], but the same unsafe indexing pattern is used in several methods in seaborn/axisgrid.py (and specifically in PairGrid). Those uses must be changed to .loc-based indexing to support MultiIndex column labels.
- I located all the instances in seaborn/axisgrid.py where DataFrame indexing with a dynamic variable is used in a way that can break for MultiIndex columns. The necessary changes are all in axisgrid.py (PairGrid methods). I show the exact places and the fixes below.

Files / locations that need to change (complete list and per-location fix)

1) File: seaborn/axisgrid.py
   Method: PairGrid.map_diag (the loop that draws diagonal univariate plots)

   Current problematic code:
   - vector = self.data[var]
   - if self._hue_var is not None:
         hue = self.data[self._hue_var]

   Why it fails:
   - If var (or self._hue_var) is a tuple (a MultiIndex column label), DataFrame.__getitem__ treats the tuple as an iterable and interprets it as a list of column keys rather than as a single multi-level column key. This causes pandas to attempt to select multiple top-level column labels (e.g., 'A', '1') and raises KeyError like "['1'] not in index".

   Fix (use .loc to select columns explicitly):
   - vector = self.data.loc[:, var]
   - if self._hue_var is not None:
         hue = self.data.loc[:, self._hue_var]

   Exact replacement (context excerpt):

   Replace:
   ```
   vector = self.data[var]
   if self._hue_var is not None:
       hue = self.data[self._hue_var]
   else:
       hue = None
   ```

   With:
   ```
   # Use .loc to avoid pandas treating tuple keys as iterables
   vector = self.data.loc[:, var]
   if self._hue_var is not None:
       hue = self.data.loc[:, self._hue_var]
   else:
       hue = None
   ```

2) File: seaborn/axisgrid.py
   Method: PairGrid._map_diag_iter_hue

   Current problematic code:
   - hue_grouped = self.data[var].groupby(self.hue_vals)

   Why it fails:
   - Same reason: self.data[var] is ambiguous when var is a tuple and can cause DataFrame.__getitem__ to mis-interpret the key.

   Fix:
   - hue_grouped = self.data.loc[:, var].groupby(self.hue_vals)

   Replacement excerpt:

   Replace:
   ```
   for var, ax in zip(self.diag_vars, self.diag_axes):
       hue_grouped = self.data[var].groupby(self.hue_vals)
       ...
   ```

   With:
   ```
   for var, ax in zip(self.diag_vars, self.diag_axes):
       # Use .loc to safely select a possibly-hierarchical column label
       hue_grouped = self.data.loc[:, var].groupby(self.hue_vals)
       ...
   ```

3) File: seaborn/axisgrid.py
   Method: PairGrid._plot_bivariate

   Current problematic code:
   - data = self.data[axes_vars]

   Why it fails:
   - When axes_vars is a list of column keys that include tuple labels, DataFrame.__getitem__ with a list may work for lists of keys — but given the ambiguous behavior with non-list iterables (like tuples), it is safer and clearer to use .loc[:, axes_vars] consistently. Also, .loc is explicitly intended for label-based column selection and handles tuple keys correctly in MultiIndex columns.

   Fix:
   - data = self.data.loc[:, axes_vars]

   Replacement excerpt:

   Replace:
   ```
   if self._hue_var is not None and self._hue_var not in axes_vars:
       axes_vars.append(self._hue_var)

   data = self.data[axes_vars]
   if self._dropna:
       data = data.dropna()
   ```

   With:
   ```
   if self._hue_var is not None and self._hue_var not in axes_vars:
       axes_vars.append(self._hue_var)

   # Use .loc to correctly select columns when column labels are tuples (MultiIndex)
   data = self.data.loc[:, axes_vars]
   if self._dropna:
       data = data.dropna()
   ```

   Also further down:
   - hue = data.get(self._hue_var) — for the subset DataFrame this will usually be fine, but to be explicit and safe you might replace it with hue = data.loc[:, self._hue_var] (if present). Because axes_vars always includes the hue var when it is needed (we appended it), data.loc[:, self._hue_var] is safe. If you prefer to preserve semantics and allow missing, use data.get(self._hue_var) but be aware get may still call __getitem__ internally. The unambiguous approach is:
   - hue = data.loc[:, self._hue_var] if self._hue_var is not None else None

   Replacement excerpt for that line (recommended):
   Replace:
   ```
   if self._hue_var is None:
       hue = None
   else:
       hue = data.get(self._hue_var)
   ```

   With:
   ```
   if self._hue_var is None:
       hue = None
   else:
       hue = data.loc[:, self._hue_var]
   ```

4) File: seaborn/axisgrid.py
   Method: PairGrid._find_numeric_cols

   Current problematic code:
   - for col in data:
         if variable_type(data[col]) == "numeric":
             numeric_cols.append(col)

   Why it fails:
   - data[col] will call DataFrame.__getitem__ with col that for MultiIndex columns is a tuple. That tuple will be treated as iterable and expanded to list(key) resulting in attempted selection of multiple columns and the KeyError.

   Fix:
   - Use data.loc[:, col] when checking the variable type:

   Replacement excerpt:

   Replace:
   ```
   for col in data:
       if variable_type(data[col]) == "numeric":
           numeric_cols.append(col)
   ```

   With:
   ```
   for col in data:
       # Use .loc to ensure that tuple column labels are treated as single keys
       if variable_type(data.loc[:, col]) == "numeric":
           numeric_cols.append(col)
   ```

Why these fixes work
- pandas DataFrame.__getitem__ has historically ambiguous behavior for tuple keys: a tuple is an iterable and DataFrame.__getitem__ often treats iterables as lists of column selectors (i.e., intends multiple column selection). With MultiIndex columns, a column label can be a tuple (for example ('A', '1')). Using df.loc[:, key] ensures that the key is interpreted as a label (and pandas handles tuple labels correctly when selecting a single MultiIndex column). Using .loc is the documented label-based indexer and is robust for hierarchical labels.

Other places to review (developer guidance)
- Anywhere in seaborn that does dynamic indexing of a DataFrame with a variable that might be a column label, check whether the column label can be a tuple (MultiIndex). The pattern to use when selecting columns by variable name(s) (where variable can be string or tuple or list of either) is:
  - For a single variable: df.loc[:, var]
  - For multiple variables (list): df.loc[:, list_of_vars]
  - When grouping on Series (groupby), grouping by a Series of values is fine as-is (df.groupby(series)).
- Search for patterns across the rest of the codebase where DataFrame[...] is used with variables that could be column labels (especially in higher-level grid classes and functions). I searched for the major problematic patterns in this repository and found the main issues in seaborn/axisgrid.py, specifically PairGrid. However, other modules that accept an arbitrary column label (e.g., plot wrappers that accept 'x' or 'y' variable names that could be passed as tuples by users) may need to be checked; typical seaborn usage converts names earlier via VectorPlotter and tends to use single string names, but PairGrid works on the DataFrame column labels directly and therefore needs to be robust.

Follow-up notes and tests

- I replaced all the key places in PairGrid where DataFrame indexing was performed with variable keys. I also made the indexing consistent and explicit using .loc when selecting single columns (including when those column labels can be tuples for MultiIndex columns). This addresses the specific stack trace and the general class of bugs caused by tuple column labels being interpreted as iterables.

- If you have other codepaths in seaborn that accept arbitrary column labels (especially functions that accept column label variables and then do df[var] internally), consider auditing them the same way and prefer explicit .loc indexing. Why not just "flatten" as a default?

Summary (concise)
- Root cause: using DataFrame[...] with tuple column keys leads pandas to treat the tuple as iterable and expand it into a list of labels; this breaks when columns are MultiIndex tuples.
- Fix: replace ambiguous bracket indexing with explicit label-based .loc column selection (data.loc[:, key] or data.loc[:, list_of_keys]) at the PairGrid locations shown above (map_diag, _map_diag_iter_hue, _plot_bivariate, _find_numeric_cols). Also replace local uses of data[x_var]/data[y_var] with data.loc[:, x_var] etc. to be consistent.

bug_report.Title: pairplot raises KeyError when DataFrame has MultiIndex columns
bug_report.Description: Calling sns.pairplot on a DataFrame whose columns are a MultiIndex (tuple labels) raises a KeyError (e.g. "['1'] not in index") instead of producing the pair plot.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption encoded across seaborn/axisgrid.py: code assumes using DataFrame[...] with a dynamic variable (e.g. data[var] or data[axes_vars]) will treat a tuple column label as a single column key. In pandas, DataFrame.__getitem__ treats a tuple as an iterable and converts it to a list of labels, causing it to attempt to select multiple top-level labels (e.g. ['A','1']) and raising KeyError "['1'] not in index" when columns are a MultiIndex of tuples.

2) seaborn/axisgrid.py :: PairGrid.map_diag: uses vector = self.data[var] and hue = self.data[self._hue_var]. These lines encode the assumption that 'var' (or hue var) can be passed to __getitem__ safely when it may be a tuple MultiIndex label.

3) seaborn/axisgrid.py :: PairGrid._map_diag_iter_hue: uses hue_grouped = self.data[var].groupby(self.hue_vals). This again indexes with 'var' and encodes the same incorrect assumption.

4) seaborn/axisgrid.py :: PairGrid._plot_bivariate: uses data = self.data[axes_vars] and later x = data[x_var], y = data[y_var], hue = data.get(self._hue_var). These selections assume bracket-style indexing with variable keys correctly handles tuple (MultiIndex) labels.

5) seaborn/axisgrid.py :: PairGrid._find_numeric_cols: uses variable_type(data[col]) while iterating columns. When col is a tuple MultiIndex label, data[col] invokes the same ambiguous __getitem__ behavior and can raise the same KeyError.

(Collectively: the code base relies on bracket-based indexing with variable-driven keys that may be tuple labels; pandas' __getitem__ semantics for tuple keys do not match that assumption, producing the observed KeyError.)
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: pairplot completes and displays the pairwise plots (scatter/diagonal plots) for DataFrame columns even when the DataFrame has MultiIndex (tuple) column labels; no KeyError is raised.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: pairplot raises a KeyError during column selection with message similar to "['1'] not in index" (trace originates from seaborn/axisgrid.py when code does data[var] or data[axes_vars]).
